The insurer declined her claim for overseas medical expenses under her travel insurance policy, stating that her bowel obstruction was a pre-existing condition and she had been diagnosed with abdominal colic more than two months before the start of her trip.
The panel concluded that there was insufficient evidence to demonstrate the woman’s bowel obstruction existed before the effective date of the travel policy, determining that the insurer should reimburse her for the medical expenses incurred in Thailand, totalling about HK$40,000.
The complaints panel aims to provide independent and impartial adjudication of claim complaints between insurers and policyholders or their beneficiaries.
The panel’s decisions are binding on all member insurers under the bureau. Complainants who disagree with a decision retain the right to pursue legal action, with their legal rights unaffected by the panel’s rulings.

data.title: Prediction markets boom raises concerns as Hong Kong calls off basketball bets
data.description:
Prediction markets are surging in popularity as cryptocurrency-based gambling platforms offering diverse gameplay and attractive odds, observers have said, warning against the risks of reneged payouts after the Hong Kong government halted plans for legalised basketball betting.
They also warned on Tuesday that Hong Kong residents who participate in these platforms, which use cryptocurrency for transactions, could be breaking gambling laws while falling into unregulated contract disputes with the events they bet on.
On Monday, authorities announced the suspension of plans for legalised basketball betting originally set to be launched in September, citing the rapid rise of prediction markets as a significant risk in attracting public attention to such platforms and indirectly fuelling the illicit market.
A prediction market treats future events like stocks and is based on crowdsourced trading. Instead of trading equity shares in a company, people buy and sell “shares” with cryptocurrencies such as USDT or ether, based on the outcome of a specific “yes-or-no event”, such as an election result or a sports competition.
Vera Yuen Wing-han, a political economy lecturer at the University of Hong Kong, said bettors gravitated towards more attractive odds and a diversity of events to gamble on, ranging from current affairs to the weather on a given day.
A check of the New York-based platform Polymarket, which has an estimated valuation of US$14.21 billion, revealed several trending bets, including wagers on whether the US and Iran would reach a permanent peace agreement by a specific date, with US$4 million at stake and live betting on five-minute price fluctuations of bitcoin.
Users also bet about US$74,000 on whether former media boss Jimmy Lai Chee-ying, who was sentenced to 20 years in prison on national security grounds in February, would be released by June 30. More than US$21,000 has been wagered on Wednesday’s highest temperature in Hong Kong.
Another market player, Kalshi, valued at US$22 billion, is running trending bets on which companies will develop the top-ranked AI models this year, and which artists will top the Spotify charts by the end of the month.
“The diversity draws people in. For example, if a person does not necessarily want to invest in the cryptocurrency market, they can simply ‘invest’ on these platforms for a different game plan,” Yuen said.
“As more money pours into a specific outcome, the odds decrease accordingly. It’s all calculated by algorithms to ensure the platform balances its total risk. Even if they lose on one specific event, the goal is to remain profitable across the entire pool of wagers.”
She added that offshore prediction markets often offered more attractive odds than, say, bets operated by the Jockey Club. The club offered more conservative odds as it was taxed by the government and faced high operational costs, from management wages to the expense of maintaining horses and facilities, she said.
Yuen cautioned that the attractive odds of offshore betting came with a lack of oversight – users had no legal recourse if a platform operating outside Hong Kong’s jurisdiction failed to honour their winnings.
She cited a controversial bet over whether US President Donald Trump would order military action against Iran. While military strikes did eventually occur, the platform refused to pay winners based on a technicality in the contract’s wording.
“The operator claimed events did not meet their specific definition of ‘entry’ because ground troops had not been deployed, even though aircraft had entered enemy airspace,” Yuen explained.
The government noted that prediction market transaction volumes reached US$64 billion in 2025 – a three-fold increase from the previous year – while monthly trading surged from under US$100 million in early 2024 to over US$13 billion by late 2025.
The Home and Youth Affairs Bureau said that this growth introduced significant uncertainty to the gambling market, with monthly volumes projected to quintuple by 2030 and over 40 per cent of betting activity expected to be sports-related.

Authorities said that wagering on sports via prediction markets was considered illegal gambling and contradicted the government’s policy of discouraging betting. It warned that rolling out basketball betting under such conditions would draw the public’s attention to such platforms and indirectly fuel the illicit market.
Lawmaker Johnny Ng Kit-chong said that these markets were a rapidly emerging sector within the blockchain world. They were packaged as predictive or financial instruments but were essentially gambling venues in which the odds were determined flexibly rather than by a traditional “house”.
“There will always be people interested in taking part in these different types of gambling activities. For instance, something like how many posts Elon Musk tweets in a week, you won’t find [markets for] those things elsewhere,” Ng said.
But he said it was difficult for local authorities to police users of prediction markets, as cryptocurrency involved private transactions between parties using digital wallets and circumvented any centralised tool that could be regulated.
“That makes it difficult for the government to investigate and gather information. I think we should step up educational efforts so that Hong Kong residents understand that gambling on these types of platforms is essentially illegal and can be prosecuted,” he said.
